fpm_inr.summary: The code implements the basic architectural components of FPM-INR (feature space decomposition, dual MLPs, differentiable forward model) but has significant deviations from the paper and is critically incomplete. The experiment script is truncated, preventing any actual training or evaluation. Key algorithmic contributions like z-slice cycling across epochs are missing, and the feature space is incorrectly split into separate instances for amplitude and phase rather than being shared.
strengths: Correct implementation of the FPM physics forward model with defocus aberration in both NumPy and PyTorch
Feature space decomposition into 2D plane M and 1D vector u with Hadamard product is conceptually implemented
Two separate MLP decoders for amplitude and phase are implemented
Random initialization of feature space parameters is correctly implemented
LED setup and illumination angle computation follow standard FPM conventions
weaknesses: Experiment script is truncated - no complete training loop, no evaluation, no comparisons are actually executed
Z-slice cycling strategy (different z-subsets per epoch) is not implemented - this is a key contribution of the paper
Feature space is incorrectly duplicated into separate instances for amplitude and phase instead of a single shared feature space
Bilinear interpolation of M is replaced with global upsampling (F.interpolate) rather than sampling at arbitrary coordinates
No patch-based processing for large field-of-view reconstruction
No real biological sample evaluation - only synthetic data
No timing, memory, or quality comparisons are actually performed
Conventional FPM reconstruction lacks embedded pupil recovery
No demonstration of continuous z-inference capability

lensless.summary: The reproduction implements the core ADMM and FISTA algorithms with RGB support and evaluation metrics (MSE, PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS), but is missing several key contributions of the paper including the abstract ReconstructionAlgorithm base class, PyTorch/GPU support, Pycsou/APGD integration, and the Hydra configuration system. The second file is truncated. The implementations that exist have some correctness concerns around padding/FFT shift consistency in ADMM.
strengths: ADMM algorithm structure with three-way splitting (data fidelity, TV, non-negativity) is correctly formulated
FISTA momentum update follows Beck & Teboulle (2009) correctly
All four evaluation metrics (MSE, PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) are properly implemented using standard libraries
RealFFTConvolve2D correctly pre-computes FFT of PSF for efficiency
RGB support is properly handled throughout the pipeline with per-channel processing
weaknesses: No abstract ReconstructionAlgorithm base class - a key design contribution of the paper
PyTorch/GPU support is entirely missing despite being a major contribution (Table 1 speedups)
Pycsou/APGD integration is missing - another key contribution for flexible regularization
The simulate_measurement function has padding logic issues (double-padding or inconsistent pad behavior)
Second file (run_dataset_experiment.py) is truncated and incomplete
No Nesterov GD or plain GD variants implemented
Potential correctness issues in ADMM _image_update where deconvolve (with ifftshift) is mixed with direct FFT operations

lfm.summary: The code correctly implements the high-level algorithmic structure of the EMS deconvolution scheme (RL/MLEM with depth-dependent anti-aliasing smoothing) and the forward/backward projection model. However, critical components—the wave-based LFPSF model, the MLA transmittance function, the anti-aliasing filter radius derivation, and the LFM parameter definitions—are delegated to missing modules (lfm_params, lfpsf), making it impossible to verify the core physics and optics of the paper. The evaluation uses only synthetic data rather than real experimental data as in the paper.
strengths: Correct implementation of the EMS algorithm structure: MLEM update followed by per-depth-slice anti-aliasing smoothing
Proper RL/MLEM update rule with precomputed sensitivity image (AT_ones)
Efficient per-slice convolution-based forward and backward projections using FFT
Reasonable experimental setup with multiple test objects and comprehensive visualization
Contrast/MTF analysis across depths attempts to reproduce paper's resolution analysis
weaknesses: Critical modules (lfm_params, lfpsf) are missing, making it impossible to verify the wave-based LFPSF model, MLA transmittance function, and anti-aliasing filter radius derivation
No real experimental data used; only synthetic test objects that may not exhibit the same aliasing artifacts as real LFM data
No MLA calibration from raw sensor images
No comparison of different LFM configurations (original vs defocused)
No evaluation on biological samples or phantoms
The super-resolution factor (deM) parameterization and its effect on PSF/volume discretization is unclear
The Lanczos2 kernel choice for anti-aliasing filter is not explicitly justified from the paper

sparse_sim.summary: The code correctly implements the core sparse deconvolution algorithm with the three-term loss function (fidelity, Hessian continuity, L1 sparsity) using FISTA optimization, which is the central methodological contribution of the paper. However, the implementation is incomplete in several important ways: it lacks most of the evaluation benchmarks described in the paper (DNA origami, Argo-SIM, ablation studies, noise level sweeps), uses only simplified Gaussian PSF models, omits temporal continuity, and the experiment code appears truncated (experiment_ring_structures is cut off). Many key validation experiments from the paper are entirely missing.
strengths: Core optimization algorithm (FISTA with L1 proximal operator and Hessian gradient) is correctly implemented
Loss function structure faithfully matches the paper's equation with all three terms
Fourier-domain 2x upsampling procedure is implemented
Background subtraction is included in the deconvolution pipeline
Synthetic image generation with band-limited OTF and realistic noise model is present
Line profile analysis with contrast measurement for resolution assessment is implemented
Soft thresholding correctly implements L1 proximal operator as specified in the paper
weaknesses: Most evaluation benchmarks from the paper are missing (DNA origami, Argo-SIM, ablation studies, noise sweeps, cross-NA validation)
No temporal continuity prior for time-lapse data
PSF model is oversimplified (Gaussian only, no Airy disk or vectorial model)
Code appears truncated - experiment_ring_structures function is incomplete
No 3D processing capability despite paper's emphasis on 3D SR imaging
No automatic parameter selection - paper likely describes methods for choosing λ and λ_L1
Only RL deconvolution is compared; Wiener, Hessian-only, and deep learning baselines are missing
No real microscopy data processing or biological structure demonstrations
No modality-specific PSF models for confocal, STED, two-photon, or SD-SIM

flfm.summary: The code implements the core FLFM forward model faithfully, including Debye PSF generation, optical Fourier transform, MLA modulation, and angular spectrum propagation. The system characterization formulas match the paper. However, the evaluation components are largely missing: no conventional LFM comparison, no artifact analysis, no timing benchmarks, and no biological sample validation. The main script appears truncated.
strengths: Correct implementation of Debye diffraction integral with Abbe-sine apodization P(θ)=cos^(1/2)(θ)
Proper optical Fourier transform with correct pixel size scaling relationship
Accurate MLA transmission function with rect amplitude mask and quadratic phase for each microlens
Multi-step angular spectrum propagation for improved accuracy
Shift-invariant 3D PSF formulation enabling efficient convolution-based Richardson-Lucy deconvolution
System characterization parameters (R_xy, R_z, FOV, N_occ) correctly computed and matching paper values
Clean code organization with both a class-based (FLFMSystem) and functional implementation
weaknesses: No conventional LFM implementation for artifact comparison - a key claim of the paper
No computational speed benchmarking against conventional LFM reconstruction
Missing iris/aperture modeling at the NIP
1:1 relay lens between MLA and camera not explicitly modeled
Main reproduction script is truncated, preventing assessment of complete evaluation pipeline
No DOF computation from the theoretical model
No biological sample or synthetic phantom validation
No demonstration of uniform aliasing properties vs conventional LFM

fpm_inr.summary: The code implements the core FPM-INR architecture reasonably well, including the feature space decomposition (2D plane + 1D vector with Hadamard product), dual MLPs for amplitude and phase, the physics-based forward model, z-slice selection strategy, and self-supervised optimization. However, the evaluation pipeline is largely incomplete or missing, pupil function recovery is not learnable, multi-patch parallel processing is absent, and the code only works with synthetic data rather than the real biological samples demonstrated in the paper.
strengths: Core INR architecture with feature decomposition (M and u with Hadamard product) is correctly implemented
Two separate MLPs for amplitude and phase as described in the paper
Physics-based FPM forward model with spectrum shifting, pupil function, defocus, and intensity measurement
Z-slice selection strategy with alternating epochs is implemented
Self-supervised optimization through the forward model without external training data
Continuous z-axis querying capability via interpolation
Conventional FPM alternating projection baseline is implemented for comparison
weaknesses: No learnable pupil function recovery / aberration correction
Evaluation pipeline is largely incomplete: no timing benchmarks, no storage comparisons, no artifact assessment
No real biological sample data processing (blood smear, thyroid lesions)
Multi-patch parallel GPU processing not implemented
Code appears truncated (compute_all_in_focus function is incomplete)
No visualization or complete experimental pipeline to reproduce paper results
Bilinear interpolation is implemented as grid upsampling rather than true arbitrary coordinate querying

lensless.summary: The code provides a functional implementation of the core reconstruction algorithms (FISTA and ADMM with TV + non-negativity) and evaluation metrics (MSE, PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) described in the paper, with both NumPy and PyTorch backends. However, it misses the key architectural contribution of the abstract ReconstructionAlgorithm class, the Pycsou/APGD integration, and several hardware-related and practical components (PSF calibration, remote display, ROI extraction).
strengths: Correct implementation of RealFFTConvolve2D with pre-computed FFTs for both NumPy and PyTorch backends
FISTA correctly implements Beck & Teboulle momentum updates with non-negativity projection
ADMM correctly implements consensus splitting with Fourier-domain x-update, non-negativity projection, and Chambolle TV proximal
All four evaluation metrics (MSE, PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) are properly implemented with correct normalization
Benchmark structure matches paper's Table 1 with correct iteration counts and GPU warmup/synchronization
RGB support is present throughout the pipeline
Measurement simulation via FFT-based convolution is correctly implemented
weaknesses: Missing the abstract ReconstructionAlgorithm class - a key architectural contribution of the paper for extensibility and code reuse
No Pycsou/APGD integration, which the paper highlights as enabling flexible regularization with arbitrary DiffFunc/ProxFunc operators
Missing plain Gradient Descent and Nesterov acceleration variants (only FISTA is implemented)
No hardware-related components: PSF calibration, remote display, ROI extraction
No Hydra configuration framework integration mentioned in the paper
PSF quality evaluation is absent
L1 and L2 sparsity regularizers shown in Figure 2 are not implemented

lfm.summary: The code implements the core algorithmic components of the paper (LFPSF computation, RL deconvolution, EMS deconvolution with anti-aliasing filters, optical system parameterization) at a structural level, but the implementation is incomplete and never runs to produce results. The main() function is truncated, no real data processing exists, the Lanczos kernel is defined but unused (replaced by Gaussian), the forward model uses shift-invariant convolution rather than the spatially-varying PSF model, and no evaluation or visualization is produced.
strengths: Correct overall structure of EMS deconvolution (E-step, M-step, S-step)
Proper MLEM/Richardson-Lucy update rule implementation with sensitivity normalization
Rayleigh-Sommerfeld propagation correctly implemented via angular spectrum method
LFM system parameterization supports both original and defocused configurations with correct derived quantities
Non-negativity constraints properly enforced
Anti-aliasing filter radius computation follows the paper's geometric analysis
weaknesses: Code is truncated/incomplete - main() never finishes, no results are produced
No real data loading or calibration - only synthetic data generation
Lanczos2 kernel is implemented but never used; Gaussian approximation substituted without justification
Forward model uses shift-invariant convolution per depth rather than spatially-varying PSF model accounting for MLA structure
Super-resolution factor (deM) is computed but volume is reconstructed at sensor resolution, not super-resolved
MLA transmittance aperture mask is ineffective due to modular arithmetic making condition always true
Debye integral uses simplified 1D radial integration with j0 rather than full 2D formulation
No evaluation, visualization, or comparison results are generated

sparse_sim.summary: The code implements a reasonable experimental framework for testing sparse deconvolution on synthetic data, with proper ablation studies and noise robustness testing. However, the core algorithm implementation (sparse_deconv.py) is not shown, many key validation components from the paper are missing (DNA origami, Argo-SIM, Fourier analysis, 3D, multi-modality), and the code is truncated. The experiments that are present follow the paper's logic but represent only a subset of the paper's comprehensive validation.
strengths: Well-structured ablation study (Experiment 4) testing sparsity-only, continuity-only, and combined priors across multiple noise levels
Proper use of pixel upsampling (×2) with appropriately rescaled PSF, matching the paper's methodology
Systematic testing across multiple structure types (filaments, rings) and separations (60-150 nm)
Noise robustness evaluation across multiple SNR levels with appropriate metrics (SSIM, PSNR, contrast)
Correct parameterization of the loss function with three weight factors matching the paper's formulation
weaknesses: Core algorithm implementation (sparse_deconv.py) is not provided, making it impossible to verify the actual optimization procedure, Hessian regularization, or ADMM implementation
No Fourier domain analysis to demonstrate bandwidth extrapolation, which is a central claim of the paper
Missing all experimental data validations: DNA origami, Argo-SIM calibration slide, live-cell imaging
No SIM raw data reconstruction pipeline - images are simply convolved with PSF rather than going through proper SIM reconstruction
No multi-modality support (SD-SIM, STED, confocal, wide-field, two-photon, ExM) despite being a key paper claim
No 3D processing or temporal continuity implementation
Code is truncated (experiment_7 is incomplete), and background estimation is trivial rather than implementing the paper's Supplementary Note 4.3 method
No Wiener deconvolution baseline comparison

flfm.summary: The reproduction correctly implements the analytical parameter framework (system characterization equations, design principle) and the FFT-based spatially-invariant deconvolution structure. However, the core wave-optics PSF model—Debye diffraction integral, MLA transmission function, and Fresnel propagation—is entirely replaced by a simplified Gaussian spot model, which fundamentally undermines the physical fidelity of the simulation. Key evaluations (artifact comparison with conventional LFM, experimental PSF validation, biological sample imaging) are absent.
strengths: Analytical system parameters (N, R_xy, R_z, FOV, DOF) are correctly computed and match paper values exactly
Design principle framework (Section 4, Table 1) is thoroughly validated with all 9 parameters checked
Spatially-invariant 3D PSF deconvolution structure using FFT-based convolution is correctly implemented
Richardson-Lucy deconvolution algorithm is properly structured with forward/back projection
Code is well-organized with clear documentation and systematic testing
weaknesses: Debye diffraction integral (Eq. 1) is completely absent—the core wave-optics PSF model is replaced by Gaussian spots with geometric parallax
MLA transmission function (rect amplitude mask + quadratic phase) and comb function are not implemented
Fresnel propagation / angular spectrum method from MLA to camera plane is missing entirely
No comparison with conventional LFM to demonstrate artifact elimination or speedup
No experimental data validation; all tests use simplified synthetic data
The simplified PSF model (Gaussian spots) cannot capture the diffraction patterns, interference effects, or depth-dependent PSF structure that are central to the paper's claims
system_params.py file is incomplete (truncated mid-comment)
